1.template: from the time that Lehi left {0}

1.question: Knowing he would soon die, what did Nephi do for his people?
options: Anointed a man to be king and ruler
Built a great temple
Divided the land among his sons
Began a new set of plates

1.title: A royal name passed down
1.text: Out of love for Nephi, every later king was called ‘second Nephi, third Nephi,’ and so on—a throne name kept regardless of the ruler’s own name, much like ‘Pharaoh’ in Egypt.

1.template: such as like unto {0} of old desiring many wives and concubines

1.question: Who consecrated Jacob and his brother Joseph as priests and teachers?
options: Nephi
Lehi
Laman
Sam

1.title: Answering for the people’s sins
1.text: Jacob and Joseph felt that if they failed to teach the word of God, the people’s sins would rest on their own heads—their garments stained with blood unless they labored with all diligence.

2.template: I come up into the {0} this day that I might declare unto you the word of God

2.title: A sermon he dreaded to give
2.text: Jacob delivers this rebuke at the temple under direct commandment, grieving that he must speak so boldly before the very wives and children most wounded by the men’s sins.

2.template: causeth me to shrink with {0} before the presence of my Maker
because of the costliness of your {0}

2.question: When the Nephites grow proud over their riches, how does God respond?
options: He condemns them and warns of swift judgments
He justifies them for their hard work
He approves if they share a little
He stays silent on the matter

2.question: What does Jacob counsel the people to seek before riches?
options: The kingdom of God
A king to rule them
More land for an inheritance
Honor among the nations

2.question: What does Jacob teach about every person before God?
options: Each soul is as precious in His sight as another
The rich are favored above the poor
Only the Nephites are precious to Him
The strong matter more than the weak

2.title: Two sins, one growing worse
2.text: Jacob first reproves the people for pride in their riches, then turns to a ‘grosser crime’—seeking to justify forbidden practices by misreading the scriptures about David and Solomon.

2.template: Behold, {0} and Solomon truly had many wives and concubines

2.question: What does the Lord command the men concerning marriage?
options: Each shall have but one wife and no concubines
They may have many wives like David
They must not marry at all
They may marry only outside their people

2.template: I, the Lord God, delight in the {0} of women

2.question: To whom does Jacob say these commandments were first given?
options: Their father Lehi
King Nephi
Jacob himself
Their brother Joseph

2.template: Ye have broken the hearts of your tender {0}

3.template: one day they shall become a {0} people

3.question: What does Jacob say about the Lamanite families?
options: Husbands and wives love each other and their children
They had forsaken all their families
They lived without any laws
They worshiped many gods

3.title: Faithful where Nephites failed
3.text: Jacob notes that the Lamanite husbands and wives loved one another and their children, keeping the one-wife commandment—making them, in this, ‘more righteous’ than the Nephites he was rebuking.

3.title: Not even a hundredth part
3.text: Jacob says a hundredth part of his people's doings could not fit on these small plates; the wars, contentions, and reigns of kings were kept on the larger plates of Nephi instead.

3.template: they were made by the hand of {0}

4.title: Why they kept the law of Moses
4.text: Jacob explains that they kept the law of Moses because it pointed their souls to Christ—and that Abraham's offering of Isaac was a similitude of God offering His Only Begotten Son.

4.question: Jacob says that by faith in Jesus' name, what would obey them?
options: The trees, the mountains, and the waves of the sea
The sun and the moon
Kings and armies
Time itself

4.template: by the power of his {0} man came upon the face of the earth
which blindness came by looking beyond the {0}

4.title: Looking beyond the mark
4.text: Jacob says the Jews stumbled by ‘looking beyond the mark’—despising the plain truths of the gospel and craving things they could not understand, until they would reject the very stone, Christ, on which they could safely build.

4.question: What does Jacob say the stone the Jews reject will become?
options: The only sure foundation on which they can build
A stumbling block forever
A wall around Jerusalem
A monument to the prophets

5.template: the words of the prophet {0}, which he spake unto the house of Israel

5.title: The allegory of the olive tree
5.text: Quoting the prophet Zenos, Jacob compares the house of Israel to a tame olive tree in a vineyard; its scattering, grafting, and gathering picture the Lord's long labor to save His covenant people.

5.template: go and pluck the branches from a {0} olive tree

5.question: What did the Lord of the vineyard do with the natural branches he took?
options: Hid them in the nethermost parts of the vineyard
Burned them in the fire
Sold them to other vineyards
Left them to wither in the sun

5.template: the wild branches have brought forth {0} fruit
it was the {0} spot in all the land of thy vineyard

5.question: When the Lord wanted to pluck the unfruitful branches, what did the servant ask?
options: To prune, dig about, and nourish it a little longer
To burn the whole tree at once
To plant a new vineyard elsewhere
To leave the vineyard forever

5.template: there are all kinds of {0} fruit

5.question: Why did the grafted tree begin to bring forth evil fruit?
options: The wild branches overran the good roots
The roots had dried up
No one watered the tree
Birds ate the good fruit

5.template: the Lord of the vineyard {0}, and said unto the servant

5.title: The Lord of the vineyard weeps
5.text: Three times the master cries ‘What could I have done more for my vineyard?’—and at one point he weeps. The allegory pictures a God who labors and grieves over His people rather than abandoning them.

5.question: What does the Lord of the vineyard insist he did NOT do?
options: Slacken his hand from nourishing the vineyard
Plant the trees himself
Send any servants to help
Taste the fruit of the trees

pairs.right: Join in a branch from another tree
Loosen the soil around the roots

5.template: those branches whose fruit is most {0}

5.question: Why does the Lord change the branches between the trees?
options: That the good may overcome the evil
To make the vineyard smaller
To sell the best fruit
To punish the servant

5.question: How many more times does the Lord say he will prune his vineyard?
options: This is the last time
Seven more times
As often as it is needed
He will never prune again

5.template: and brought other servants; and they were {0}

5.question: Who labored in the vineyard alongside the servants this last time?
options: The Lord of the vineyard himself
Only hired strangers
The wild branches
No one—they worked alone

5.title: He labors beside His servants
5.text: In the final gathering the Lord of the vineyard does not merely send servants—he labors with them. The few who work with their might share His joy in the good fruit at last preserved.

5.question: At the end, what will the Lord do when evil fruit returns?
options: Gather the good and bad, preserve the good, and burn the vineyard
Abandon the vineyard entirely
Graft in more wild branches
Sell the vineyard to strangers

6.title: Jacob's own prophecy
6.text: Having quoted Zenos's long allegory, Jacob adds his own short prophecy: the gathering of Israel it describes—the Lord setting his hand ‘the second time’—will surely come to pass before the world is burned.

6.template: he remembereth the house of Israel, both roots and {0}

6.question: What does Jacob plead with the people not to reject?
options: The words spoken concerning Christ and the plan of redemption
The traditions of their fathers
The reigns of their kings
The riches of the land

6.template: continue in the way which is {0}

6.title: The pleasing bar of God
6.text: Jacob ends with a phrase found only in his writings—he hopes to meet his readers at the ‘pleasing bar of God,’ the same judgment that ‘striketh the wicked with awful dread.’

7.template: there came a man among the people of Nephi, whose name was {0}

7.title: The first named anti-Christ
7.text: Sherem is the first of the Book of Mormon's named ‘anti-Christs’—a learned, persuasive man who used flattery and ‘much power of speech’ to deny that Christ would come and to lead hearts away.

7.template: keep not the law of {0} which is the right way

7.question: How did Sherem answer when Jacob asked if he denied the Christ who should come?
options: He claimed there is no Christ, nor ever will be
He admitted Christ would surely come
He refused to give any answer
He said he would wait and see

7.template: if there should be no {0} made all mankind must be lost

7.question: What did Sherem demand of Jacob?
options: A sign by the power of the Holy Ghost
Half of his possessions
To leave the land of Nephi
A public debate before the king

7.question: After he was smitten, what did Sherem tell the multitude?
options: He confessed Christ and that the devil had deceived him
He repeated that there is no Christ
He blamed Jacob for his sickness
He asked to be made their king

7.question: After Sherem's death, what happened among the people?
options: Peace and the love of God were restored, and they searched the scriptures
They scattered into the wilderness
They chose Sherem's followers as leaders
They abandoned the law of Moses

7.template: trusting in the God and {0} of their salvation
I said unto my son {0}: Take these plates

pairs.right: Denied Christ, then confessed Him before dying
Jacob's son, who received the plates
Jacob's brother, who began the plates

7.title: ‘Brethren, adieu’
7.text: Jacob closes by handing the plates to his son Enos and bids his readers ‘adieu’—a word borrowed into English from French that means, fittingly, ‘to God.’
